title: Perfect Nonreciprocal Axion-polaritons
abstract: Dynamical axion describes a massive axion quasiparticle that arises from fluctuations of the magnetic order parameter. These fluctuations couple to electromagnetic fields, forming axion-polaritons -- collective modes that are physically magnon-polaritons. We show that under appropriate static external electric and magnetic fields, axion-polaritons acquire a nonreciprocal dispersion, $ω(\textbf{k}) \neq ω(-\textbf{k})$, arising intrinsically from direction-dependent axion-photon coupling. Strikingly, we identify a regime of \textit{perfect nonreciprocity} in which the photon propagating in one direction is completely decoupled from the axion while the counter-propagating photon hybridizes strongly. Furthermore, the nonreciprocal dispersion manifests as an optical isolator -- a device transmitting light preferentially in one direction. Our results establish nonreciprocal axion-polaritons as a powerful probe of axion quasiparticles and suggest experimentally accessible routes for their detection.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates axion-polaritons in a dynamical axion insulator subjected to external electric and magnetic fields. It demonstrates that these systems exhibit intrinsic nonreciprocity, leading to the prediction of an optical isolator. The effect is realized in a 'perfect nonreciprocity' regime where one direction of light propagates freely while the opposite direction is strongly attenuated.
main_problem:
The paper aims to characterize nonreciprocal axion-polaritons, which are hybrid modes exhibiting directional asymmetry in their dispersion relation ($\omega(k) 
eq \omega(-k)$).
main_result: They identify a regime of perfect nonreciprocity where one direction's photon decouples from the axion while the counter-propagating photon strongly hybridizes, realizing an optical isolator.
method: The analysis involves solving coupled equations of motion for axion and photon fields, deriving dispersion relations, and calculating transmission/reflection coefficients using boundary value problems.
model_or_system: The system is a dynamical axion insulator coupled to electromagnetic fields, described by a Lagrangian involving axion fluctuations ($	heta$) and photon fields ($A$). Nonreciprocity requires breaking both inversion and time-reversal symmetry using external $\mathbf{E}_0$ and $\mathbf{B}_0$ fields.
key_observables: Dispersion relation $\omega(k)$, group velocity asymmetry $|\mathbf{v}_g(\mathbf{k})|$, and transmission coefficients $|t_{LR}|^2$ vs. $|t_{RL}|^2$.
important_parameters: The perfect nonreciprocity limit is achieved when the external fields satisfy $E = c'B$. The analysis also considers the axion damping rate $\Gamma$.
main_assumptions: Approximations include $v \ll c'$ and the use of a small dissipative term ($\Gamma$) to model finite axion quasiparticle lifetime.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the coupling mechanism showing different coupling strengths for right- and left-moving photons, and show the calculated transmission coefficients $|t_{LR}|^2$ and $|t_{RL}|^2$ for both nonreciprocal ($E=c'B$) and reciprocal ($E=0$) cases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the coupled equations and the requirement for nonreciprocity, to calculating the dispersion relation and coupling strengths, and finally solving the boundary value problem for transmission/reflection coefficients to demonstrate the optical isolator effect.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a controllable, engineered mechanism for directional light control (optical isolation) using fundamental material properties (axion coupling).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. (a) Perfect nonreciprocity at critical electromagnetic fields Ec 0, Bc 0 given by Ec 0 = c′Bc 0. (b) The axion-polariton dispersion at the critical external field. (c) The axion-photon coupling strengths ga(k) as a function of momentum k at critical electromagnetic fields Ec 0, Bc 0. We use E = c′B, B = 1.5 GHz, m = 40 GHz, v = 0.005 and c′ = 0.3.
FIG. 3. (a) The axion insulator as an optical isolator. (b) The transmissivities |tLR|2 and |tRL|2 as a function of photon frequency ω. We use E = c′B , B = 1.5 GHz, m = 40 GHz, v = 0 and c′ = 0.3.
